---
title: "3.7 Class Variables and Static Counters"
subtitle: "Class Variables and Methods"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Class Variables

### Belong to the class

**Class variables** belong to the class, with all objects of a class sharing a single copy of the class variable.

Class variables are designated with the `static` keyword before the variable type.

Class variables that are designated `public` are accessed outside of the class by using the class name and the dot operator, since they are associated with a class, not objects of a class.

## Static Counter Context

### `Person.personCounter`

In the following class `Person`, there is a `static` variable called `personCounter`.

It is incremented each time the `Person` constructor is called to initialize a new `Person` object.

The static method `printCounter` prints out its value.

The Java visualizer link from the source is omitted here; the trace is kept as classroom work.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: PersonClassStaticCounter`

What will the following code print out?

Try adding another `Person` object and see what happens.

Try running the code step by step and trace when `personCounter` changes.

## PersonClassStaticCounter Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Person
{
    // instance variables
    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phoneNumber;

    // Static counter variable
    public static int personCounter = 0;

    // static method to print out counter
    public static void printPersonCounter()
    {
        System.out.println("Person counter: " + personCounter);
    }

    // constructor: construct a Person copying in the data into the instance
    // variables
    public Person(String initName, String initEmail, String initPhone)
    {
        name = initName;
        email = initEmail;
        phoneNumber = initPhone;
        personCounter++;
    }

    // toString() method
    public String toString()
    {
        return name + ": " + email + " " + phoneNumber;
    }

    // main method for testing
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        // call the constructor to create a new person
        Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com", "123-456-7890");
        Person p2 = new Person("Jean", "jean@gmail.com", "404 899-9955");

        Person.printPersonCounter();
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### Runestone check

The expected output from `main` is:

```text
Person counter: 2
```

The count is `2` because the constructor ran twice.

If you add another `Person` object, the same shared `personCounter` should increase again.

## Static Variable Use

### Shared values across objects

Another common use for static variables is to keep track of a minimum value, maximum value, or average of values in a collection of objects.

The next check traces a static `maxTemp` value shared by all `Temperature` objects.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: staticTrace`

Consider the class `Temperature` below which has a static variable.

What is the output of the `main` method?

```java
public class Temperature
{
    private double temperature;
    public static double maxTemp = 0;

    public Temperature(double t)
    {
        temperature = t;
        if (t > maxTemp)
        {
             maxTemp = t;
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Temperature t1 = new Temperature(75);
        Temperature t2 = new Temperature(100);
        Temperature t3 = new Temperature(65);
        System.out.println("Max Temp: " + Temperature.maxTemp);
    }
}
```

## Answer Choices

### Static trace options

::: {.tight-list}
- A. `Max Temp: 0`
- B. compiler error because the static variable `maxTemp` cannot be used inside a non-static constructor
- C. `Max Temp: 100`
- D. `Max Temp: 75`
- E. `Max Temp: 65`
:::

## Answer Reasoning

### Correct answer: C

`maxTemp` is initialized to `0`.

It is changed to `75` by the first constructor call, then to `100` by the second constructor call.

It is not changed to `65` by the third constructor call because `65` is not greater than `100`.

The output is:

```text
Max Temp: 100
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: TemperatureBugs`

Fix the bugs in the following code.

Static methods should only access static variables.

The source visualizer link is omitted, but the code and fix target are preserved.

## TemperatureBugs Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Temperature
{
    private double temperature;
    public static double maxTemp = 0;

    public Temperature(double t)
    {
        temperature = t;
        if (t > maxTemp)
        {
             maxTemp = t;
        }
    }
    // This static method should print out the static variable
    // TODO: Fix the method header so it has a return type
    public static printMax()
    {
        // TODO: Print the static variable maxTemp, not the instance variable
        System.out.println(temperature);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Temperature t1 = new Temperature(75);
        Temperature t2 = new Temperature(100);
        Temperature.printMax();
    }
}
```
:::

## Focused Fix

### Static method header and static field

The `printMax` method needs a complete static method header:

```java
public static void printMax()
```

Inside that static method, print the static variable, not the instance variable.

## Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The tests check for:

::: {.tight-list}
- `public static void printMax()`
- either `System.out.println(maxTemp);` or `System.out.println(Temperature.maxTemp);`
- expected output from `main`: `100.0`
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define a class variable as one shared copy for the class
- explain why `personCounter` changes in the constructor
- trace `Person counter: 2` from two constructor calls
- trace `Temperature.maxTemp` across three objects
- explain why static methods should not directly access instance variables
- fix `TemperatureBugs` by printing `maxTemp` from `printMax`
:::

## End

### 3.7 Part 2 complete

Next: `final` constants and the Static Song challenge.
